problem_summary: 2011 MCM Problem A (continuous): design the shape of a snowboard halfpipe course that maximizes "vertical air" (the height a snowboarder rises above the halfpipe edge), then optimize the shape for other trick requirements and decide which idealized properties to abandon for a practical course. Team 9159 reframes "max vertical air" as "maximize the snowboarder's final mechanical energy E_t at the midpoint/coping after one transit," and derives the course geometry that minimizes net energy loss (friction + inelastic-collision + air resistance) relative to gravitational energy gain.
models_used: Energy-balance ODE model: a first-order linear ODE in mechanical energy E along the curve, dE/dx + [2*mu*phi''/(1+phi'^2)]*E = -mu*m*g*[1 - 2*phi*phi''/(1+phi'^2)], derived from dE = -mu*N*ds with normal force N = m*g*cos(theta) + m*v^2/rho (gravity + centripetal) and curvature 1/rho = phi''/(1+phi'^2)^{3/2}

Closed-form (integrating-factor) solution of the energy ODE giving E[phi,W] as a functional of the unknown course curve phi(x); the friction integral collapses analytically to arctan(phi') = theta via integral of phi''/(1+phi'^2)

Perfect-inelastic-collision landing model: at landing point (x0, phi(x0)) the rider loses the velocity component normal to the snow surface, setting the ODE initial condition E_0[phi] = m*g*y0 + m*g*(h-y0)*phi'(x0)^2/(1+phi'(x0)^2)
Quarter-circle transition model (2D): reduces phi to a single parameter R, yielding a fully analytical E(w) expression and an optimal radius R* (3.83 m for the nominal parameters)

Quarter-ellipse transition model (2D): two-parameter family (semi-major a, semi-minor b), solved numerically for optimal (a*, b*) = (4.19, 5.4) m, layered in three stages: friction only, friction+collision, friction+collision+air resistance
Air-resistance extension: adds a linear-in-velocity drag term -alpha*A*v*sqrt(1+phi'^2) to the ODE (damping coefficient alpha, windward area A)

3D inclined-halfpipe model: a coordinate transformation (x', y') mapping the downhill-sloped pipe back to the 2D problem, letting gravity inject energy; solves for optimal slope angle phi* = 17.1 degrees
Sensitivity model: dimensionless sensitivity coefficient s = (dA/dB)/(A/B)|_{B0} computed for a* against x0, h_i, alpha, h0, mu, E_i, and slope angle
key_techniques: Reformulating an ill-posed geometry-optimization problem as energy maximization, making 'vertical air' a single scalar objective E_t
Integrating-factor solution of a linear first-order ODE, exploiting the identity integral of phi''/(1+phi'^2) dx = arctan(phi') = theta to get a clean closed form

Dimensionality reduction to escape a hard calculus-of-variations problem: instead of optimizing over the full function space of phi(x), restrict phi to parametric families (circle -> 1 param R; ellipse -> 2 params a,b) and optimize over those few parameters
Forward Euler integration (step 10^-4 m) of the energy ODE for the ellipse case, with bisection to locate optimal a*, b*
Progressive model layering: build a minimal model first (friction + collision), then add air resistance, then add the third dimension/slope — each layer isolates one effect
Dimensionless sensitivity coefficients to rank-order which parameters (mu, h0, h_i) actually drive the optimum versus which (alpha) are negligible

Physical anchoring of every parameter to cited real-world standards (FIS halfpipe dimensions w~9 m, h0=5.4 m; mu in 0.08-0.11; edge angle 83-88 deg), then validating outputs against actual competition geometry
Cubic-spline interpolation of the h_f-vs-phi data to solve for the slope angle where hang-heights on both walls match (h_f = 9.0 m -> phi* = 17.1 deg)

Theory-to-practice 'tailoring' pass: deliberately deviating from the energy-optimal design (e.g. keeping the flat bottom, lowering edge angle to ~85 deg, nudging slope to 17.5 deg) to satisfy safety, construction, and trick-twist constraints
why_it_won: From a judge's perspective this paper wins because it nails the three things judges reward most: (1) a clean, defensible central idea — 'measure vertical air by final mechanical energy' — that turns a vague design brief into a rigorous optimization with one scalar objective; (2) genuine analytical depth — they derive a real ODE from first-principles force analysis (gravity + centripetal normal force, curvature, inelastic-collision boundary condition) and solve it in closed form via integrating factor, rather than hand-waving or jumping straight to a black-box simulation; and (3) demonstrable validity — the model's outputs (slope ~17 deg, edge angle ~85 deg, elliptical transition, b* at full pipe height) independently reproduce real FIS competition halfpipe geometry, which is the single most persuasive evidence a judge can see. The escalation from analytical quarter-circle -> numerical quarter-ellipse -> 3D sloped pipe shows mathematical maturity and lets them present both an exact result and a refined one. The quantitative sensitivity analysis and the explicit 'tailors and tradeoffs' section (safety, construction, twists) prove they understood that the real deliverable is a buildable course, not a math toy. Together these give the paper the trademark Outstanding profile: rigorous, validated, well-scoped, and connected to reality.
section_highlights: The Summary Sheet is a model of clarity: it states the core idea ('measure vertical air by final energy'), lists the assumption-layering strategy, and previews every result (R*, a*, b*, slope angle) in one page. The Basic Model section (Sec 3) is the analytical centerpiece — a tight derivation from dE = -mu*N*ds to the governing ODE Eq.(5) to the closed-form functional Eq.(8/11), with the elegant arctan(phi')=theta collapse. The 2D Application section is structured as a clean three-step ablation (friction only / +collision / +air), each with paired E_t-vs-a and E_t-vs-b figures, so the reader sees exactly what each physical effect does (notably the insight that ignoring collision drives the trail to a degenerate right angle, proving collision is essential). The Sensitivity section (Sec 4.3) converts qualitative claims into ranked numbers (e.g. s=2.374 for h0, s=0.011 for alpha), which is unusually disciplined. The 'Tailors and Tradeoffs' section is the strategic high point — it consciously walks the optimum back toward reality for safety/construction/trick reasons, and the honest Strengths/Weaknesses section (admitting the mass-point and fixed-velocity-direction limitations) builds credibility. Heavy, consistent use of figures throughout makes the parameter relationships legible.
pitfalls_or_limitations: Mass-point assumption discards exactly the physics that produces 'vertical air' in real snowboarding — riders actively pump, extend/retract, and add energy via leg work ('pumping'), so a pure passive-energy-loss model structurally cannot capture how real height above the coping is generated (the authors acknowledge this but it undercuts the objective's fidelity)
Velocity direction assumed fixed and motion confined to a 2D plane (acknowledged weakness); real tricks involve out-of-plane rotation and path changes

The objective conflates 'final mechanical energy E_t at the coping' with 'vertical air height' — these are related but the mapping from exit energy to airborne height above the edge is never explicitly derived, so the optimized quantity is a proxy

Restricting phi(x) to circle/ellipse families is a convenience, not a proof of optimality — the authors admit the ellipse 'may not be the best curve'; the true variational optimum over all curves is left open

Several figures are inconsistent/likely mislabeled: Fig 13 (a*-h0) and Fig 11 (a*-h_i) show identical data tables, and Figs 17/18/19 share an identical, partly wrong caption ('snow friction and collision considered'), suggesting rushed figure production

Some numerical results look internally inconsistent (e.g. Fig 7 left shows E_t rising from a=1 to a=10 while the text says collision-free optimum pushes a->0; sensitivity sign conventions for h_i/E_i are stated as negative without clear derivation)

Air-resistance term is shown to be nearly negligible (s=0.011), so a meaningful fraction of the added modeling effort yields little — a reviewer could argue it was included for completeness rather than insight
Parameter sourcing leans on weak references (Wikipedia, a Chinese weather encyclopedia baike.weather.com.cn for the wind/damping scale), which slightly weakens the empirical grounding
reusable_patterns: Objective reframing: when a design/shape problem is vaguely stated ('make it as high/fast/good as possible'), recast it as optimizing a single physically meaningful scalar (here, final mechanical energy) — this makes the whole problem tractable and gives a clear thing to plot

Escape calculus-of-variations by parametrization: when the unknown is a whole function and a direct variational attack is intractable, restrict it to a low-dimensional parametric family (circle->ellipse->...) and optimize the few parameters; present the simplest family analytically and the richer family numerically

Assumption-layering / ablation structure: start from the minimal model, then add one effect at a time (collision, then drag, then 3D slope), each with its own figure, so each factor's contribution is isolated and the writing self-documents the modeling decisions
Analytical-then-numerical ladder: get a closed-form result for the simplest case (gives intuition and a sanity check), then switch to forward-Euler + bisection for the case that resists closed form

Dimensionless sensitivity coefficient s = (dA/dB)/(A/B) to quantitatively rank which inputs matter — a cheap, high-credibility addition that turns 'we considered many factors' into 'here is exactly how much each one moves the answer'

Validation-by-real-data: anchor every parameter to published standards and then show the model's optimum independently reproduces real-world values (slope ~17 deg, edge angle ~85 deg) — the most persuasive evidence available to judges

Explicit theory-to-practice 'tailoring' section: after finding the mathematical optimum, deliberately and transparently adjust it for real constraints (safety, buildability, secondary goals), documenting each tradeoff — directly answers the 'what to abandon' style sub-prompt and shows engineering judgment
Coordinate transformation to reuse a lower-dimensional model: map the harder 3D inclined problem back onto the already-solved 2D problem via (x',y') substitution rather than re-deriving from scratch
Honest Strengths/Weaknesses section that names the load-bearing assumptions (mass point, fixed velocity direction, non-optimal curve family) — builds reviewer trust rather than hiding limitations
